title: Systemic corticosteroids and mortality in critically ill patients with COVID-19: a meta-analysis of randomized trials
citation: Modeled on the WHO REACT Working Group prospective meta-analysis (Sterne JAC et al., JAMA 2020;324(13):1330-1341). Per-study numbers are representative test data structured to match the review's design (pooled odds ratio for 28-day mortality across RCTs); they are NOT verbatim transcriptions. Grounding is evaluated against the source text in this fixture, so the values are internally consistent and replaceable with real extracted PDF text.

protocol.researchQuestion: In critically ill adults with COVID-19, does systemic corticosteroid therapy compared with usual care or placebo reduce 28-day all-cause mortality?

values.P: Critically ill hospitalized adults with COVID-19

template.name: Corticosteroid RCT outcomes
template.instructions: Extract the reported treatment-effect odds ratio with its 95% confidence interval for each outcome, plus the total number of participants analyzed and the number of events.

outcomes.name: 28-day all-cause mortality
outcomes.description: Odds of all-cause death by 28 days, corticosteroid vs control.

outcomes.name: Serious adverse events
outcomes.description: Odds of a serious adverse event, corticosteroid vs control.

studies.label: RECOVERY (dexamethasone)
studies.source: RECOVERY trial, critically ill subgroup. In this randomized controlled trial, a total of 403 critically ill patients with COVID-19 receiving invasive mechanical ventilation were analyzed, and 116 deaths occurred by 28 days. Dexamethasone was associated with lower 28-day all-cause mortality compared with usual care (odds ratio 0.66; 95% CI, 0.51 to 0.85). No excess of serious adverse events was attributed to the steroid arm.

studies.label: REMAP-CAP (hydrocortisone)
studies.source: REMAP-CAP corticosteroid domain. Among 324 critically ill adults with COVID-19 in the intensive care unit who were randomized to hydrocortisone or no hydrocortisone, 95 deaths were recorded at 28 days. Hydrocortisone was associated with reduced 28-day all-cause mortality (odds ratio 0.64; 95% CI, 0.42 to 0.97). Serious adverse events were uncommon: among the 324 patients, 64 serious adverse events were reported, with no significant difference between groups (odds ratio 1.20; 95% CI, 0.80 to 1.79).

studies.label: CoDEX (dexamethasone)
studies.source: CoDEX trial. A total of 299 patients with COVID-19 and moderate-to-severe acute respiratory distress syndrome were analyzed, with 88 deaths by 28 days. Dexamethasone showed an association with lower 28-day all-cause mortality (odds ratio 0.70; 95% CI, 0.48 to 1.01).

studies.label: CAPE COVID (hydrocortisone)
studies.source: CAPE COVID trial. In this randomized study, 149 critically ill patients with COVID-19 were analyzed and 41 deaths occurred at 28 days. Low-dose hydrocortisone was associated with reduced 28-day all-cause mortality, although the estimate was imprecise (odds ratio 0.69; 95% CI, 0.40 to 1.19).

studies.label: Steroids-SARI (methylprednisolone)
studies.source: Steroids-SARI trial. Among 197 critically ill patients with COVID-19 analyzed, 60 deaths were observed by day 28. Methylprednisolone was associated with lower 28-day all-cause mortality (odds ratio 0.80; 95% CI, 0.48 to 1.33).
